5-5.title: Freedom Riders Depart Washington, D.C. on First Interstate Journey
5-5.summary: On May 5, 1961, thirteen Freedom Riders — seven Black and six white — organized by the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) departed Washington, D.C. aboard two interstate buses bound for New Orleans, directly challenging the Supreme Court's Boynton v. Virginia (1960) ruling being ignored across the South. Participants including John Lewis and James Farmer knowingly risked violent retaliation to force federal enforcement of desegregated interstate travel facilities.
5-5.chain: The savage mob attacks on the Freedom Riders in Anniston and Birmingham, Alabama days later forced the Kennedy administration to order the Interstate Commerce Commission to issue enforceable desegregation regulations (ICC Order, November 1961), directly dismantling the Jim Crow infrastructure of interstate travel and demonstrating that sustained nonviolent direct action could compel federal intervention — a strategic model that shaped every major civil rights campaign through the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
